text: 30MHz is an Amsterdam-based software company who set out to build an analytics and management
platform for smart devices. They never dreamed they would end up in agriculture.
We started with a cloud infrastructure monitoring platform. We developed this on AWS.
I'm Jasper Geurtze, co-founder and head of hardware department in 30MHz.
The first few years we focused on building the product and finding our industry.
My name is Flaa Baghanelli. I am co-founder of 30MHz and former CTO. We knew that we wanted to
combine our strong AWS infrastructure with wireless sensors to digitalize some process.
But we didn't know what process yet.
30MHz found the ideal match for that combination and the agricultural industry, specifically
greenhouses and indoor farms.
We realized that we were culturally a very good fit. And then we decided to focus on building
the product knowing that we would add value to the space.
With the global population predicted to reach 9.1 billion by 2050, the world's food production
needs to increase by at least 70 percent. Meaning more farmers grow more food for more people,
while not taxing our planet's precious resources.
We set out to make a data-driven technology for any farmers in the world, for any states. We knew when
we came into the agriculture field that we had technology that could really help low-tech farmers
bridge the gap with high-tech farmers.
The original 30MHz platform called and analyzed data from infield monitoring devices, allowing
farmers to remotely monitor and manage much of what happened in their greenhouses.
What you see is that farmers are cultivating an ever-increasing plot of land. And with descriptive
and predictive analytics, we can help them manage these areas more effectively.
I'm Dam Rutten, I'm a data scientist working for 30MHz.
In the early days, teams had to troubleshoot outages, loss of data, and found the monitoring
system difficult to scale.
So we had to redesign part of our architecture to make our gateways run autonomously. But we saw this
as an opportunity to drop some of our software and start using AWS IoT.
With the support of the AWS prototype team, we managed to integrate AWS Greengrass into our gateways.
And this way we loosen the boundary between the edge and the cloud network and really make
this flexible. And we get additional features like offline buffering. So when the connection drops,
then Greengrass will store the data for us. And when the connection is recovered, it will send out the missing time.

My name is Amir Majlesi. I look after AWS prototype activities in the Benelux and Nordics. We collaborated with the 30MHz team in a hands-on rapid prototyping engagement, helping them modernize their architecture and build serverless, smart agriculture system.
We co-developed a working prototype which was customized to their business environment in just three weeks.

The new prototype solved the device management and connectivity issues and helped 30MHz automate the provisioning of gateways. It also added capabilities such as over-the-air updates and advanced security measures to protect data.
In 2019, the team participated in the autonomous greenhouse challenge, sponsored by Wackenden University and Research.
The goal of the greenhouse challenge was to autonomously manage a greenhouse for six months. We were cultivating tomatoes.
The experience pushed 30MHz to look closer at how they could bring this type of data analytics and machine learning to real growers.
And I think it was the introduction to the challenge that also introduced us to the possibilities for autonomous growing for actual greenhouses.
30MHz sought to develop a solution that combined the data monitoring software and the data collecting hardware with machine learning.
This machine learning technology would enable growers to make real-time decisions on climate and irrigation, creating optimal growing conditions for crops.
The 30MHz team had highly specialized expertise in digital agriculture and data science.
The prototype engagements really built upon these existing skills and enabled the team to develop a new machine learning pipeline with Amazon SageMaker.
The AWS team helped 30MHz analyze the data, build and deploy the machine learning models for inference, and automate the machine learning process from beginning to end.
We implemented the architecture in two parts, model training and inference.
First, we fetched the existing 30MHz codes from their code repository.
We train this model with Amazon SageMaker using specific datasets from each grower.
Once training is done, SageMaker stores the model artifacts in an S3 bucket.
The next step is to evaluate the model performance to check if the accuracy is sufficient enough.
After it passes, the model is deployed.
Once deployed, the model is now ready for inference with incoming sensor data collected from customer fields using AWS Greengrass.
One of our customers is an IPM consultant.
He has been using the 30MHz platform to really look into the data with grower groups on situations where pests and diseases might increase.
And analyzing this microclimate data, including the crop crop data, to really see situations or conditions that may explain these kinds of situations.
For these grower groups, understanding and visualizing this data on the disease management is really helping them to push back the infection.
They're not only safe money, but it's maybe more important that they're really able to leave out some chemical solutions that might otherwise be necessary to really push back the disease.
So we envision our technology being available everywhere in the world at a fraction of the cost of climate computer systems.
And this will dramatically lower the costs of running a greenhouse.
This can happen everywhere in the world.
The 30MHz prototype is currently being tested by eight pilot growers, with the hopes of scaling up to hundreds, even thousands of growers in the near future.
The insights and information that this technology gives to our customers can be life changing.
The collaboration with AWS prototyping has been key for this because we have been able to build and test fast and also create solutions which are scalable and reliable from the beginning.
The 30MHz team continues to look to the future, refining and expanding the capabilities while keeping the technology easy to use.
I think one of the things that we've experienced is that it's possible to have real-time autonomous control.
The main challenge, however, is to incorporate real-time feedback from the crop, which we still aren't able to measure, or at least measure to the extent that we'd like.
Today, 30MHz has 35 employees and services over 330 customers across 30 countries and counting.
30MHz wants to be the leader who brings the infrastructure and information to growers to allow them and the whole industry to advance at a much faster pace.
30MHz can be the leader who can make better use of resources and feed the world.
